adult-36-uri.name: T. Chen
adult-36-uri.chiefComplaint: I've had this cough and congestion for about a week

missedConsequences.Pneumonia: Missed pneumonia leads to worsening respiratory status. Patient presents to ER 3 days later with respiratory failure requiring ICU admission.
missedConsequences.Inappropriate antibiotic use: Prescribing antibiotics for viral URI contributes to antibiotic resistance, causes unnecessary side effects (C. diff, allergic reactions), and doesn't help the patient.

commonPitfalls: Prescribing antibiotics for viral URI because 'the patient expects them' - this is harmful to the patient and society
Thinking yellow/green sputum means bacterial infection - COLOR DOES NOT INDICATE BACTERIA
Diagnosing 'bacterial sinusitis' without meeting criteria: >10 days OR double-worsening OR severe symptoms
Not educating patients about the natural course of viral URI (7-10 days is normal!)
Missing pneumonia by not doing a proper lung exam in patients with cough
Forgetting to ask about red flag symptoms: high fever, dyspnea, hypoxia, chest pain

mcqs.question: The patient's sputum is yellow. They ask if this means they need an antibiotic. What is the correct response?

options.text: Yes, yellow/green sputum indicates bacterial infection requiring antibiotics
options.explanation: MYTH! Sputum color does not reliably distinguish viral from bacterial infection. Yellow/green color comes from neutrophils (white blood cells), which respond to ANY inflammation.

options.text: No, sputum color does not reliably indicate bacterial infection
options.explanation: Correct. Multiple studies show sputum color does not predict bacterial vs. viral etiology. Prescribing antibiotics based on sputum color promotes antibiotic resistance without benefiting the patient.

options.text: Maybe - need to culture the sputum first
options.explanation: Sputum cultures are rarely helpful in outpatient respiratory infections and wouldn't change management here.

options.text: Yes, but only if it's been yellow for more than 5 days
options.explanation: Duration of colored sputum doesn't change the fact that color alone doesn't indicate bacterial infection.

mcqs.question: The patient has had symptoms for 7 days with facial pressure. Their exam is reassuring. They're frustrated and ask 'When does this become a sinus infection that needs antibiotics?' What are the criteria for bacterial sinusitis?

options.text: Any sinus pressure lasting more than 3 days
options.explanation: This is too short. Viral sinusitis commonly lasts 7-10 days.

options.text: Symptoms >10 days WITHOUT improvement, OR 'double worsening', OR severe symptoms
options.explanation: Bacterial sinusitis criteria: (1) Symptoms >10 days without improvement, (2) 'Double worsening' - improving then getting worse again, or (3) Severe symptoms (fever ≥102°F, purulent nasal discharge for 3+ days). This patient meets NONE of these criteria.

options.text: Colored nasal discharge for any duration
options.explanation: Color doesn't indicate bacterial infection. Purulent discharge must be combined with other criteria.
Facial pressure is common with viral URI and doesn't indicate bacterial infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection (URI)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: gradual onset, nasal congestion, postnasal drip, low-grade fever, clear/yellow sputum, 7-day duration with improvement. Sick contacts present.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Bronchitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Persistent cough with sputum, worse at night. Often post-viral. Distinction from URI is somewhat arbitrary - both are usually viral.

allDiagnoses.name: Bacterial Sinusitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Facial pressure, headache worse bending forward, 7-day duration. However, no high fever, no purulent drainage, no 'double worsening' - criteria for bacterial not met.

allDiagnoses.name: Pneumonia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out. Would expect higher fever, productive cough with purulent sputum, dyspnea, focal lung findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Dangerous complication of pharyngitis. Would expect severe sore throat, trismus, 'hot potato' voice, uvula deviation.

allDiagnoses.name: Epiglottitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Medical emergency. Would expect rapid onset, high fever, drooling, stridor, respiratory distress.

allDiagnoses.name: Influenza
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect more systemic symptoms: high fever, myalgias, sudden onset, severe fatigue.

allDiagnoses.name: Allergic Rhinitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Patient has seasonal allergies but says 'this feels different.' Low-grade fever and sick contacts point to infection.

allDiagnoses.name: COVID-19
allDiagnoses.rationale: Should be considered in any respiratory illness. Would confirm with testing if concerned.

adult-36-uri.oldcartsNarrative: "It started about a week ago with a scratchy throat and stuffy nose. Then it got worse over a couple days - lots of congestion, postnasal drip, and this cough that won't quit. The cough brings up clear or slightly yellow stuff, especially in the mornings. I feel this pressure under my eyes and get headaches, especially when I bend forward. No real fever - maybe 99-100 at the highest. My ears feel kind of plugged but not painful. I've been taking DayQuil and NyQuil and some saline spray which helps a little. My kid had a cold last week so I probably caught it from them. I get seasonal allergies but this feels different. I'm worried it might be turning into something worse like pneumonia."

historyQuestions.text: How did this start and how has it progressed?
historyQuestions.answer: Started about a week ago with a scratchy throat and stuffy nose. Got worse over the next 2-3 days, then kind of plateaued. The sore throat actually went away but the congestion and cough are hanging on.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic URI pattern: gradual onset, peaks at day 3-4, then slow improvement. 'Double worsening' (improving then getting worse again) would suggest bacterial superinfection.

historyQuestions.text: What does the sputum look like when you cough?
historyQuestions.answer: Mostly clear, sometimes a little yellow. Not green or bloody.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Clear/yellow sputum is typical of viral URI. Green doesn't necessarily mean bacterial. Rusty or bloody sputum would raise concern for pneumonia.

historyQuestions.text: What's the highest your temperature has gotten?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe 99 or 100 at the highest. Never got really high.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-grade fever typical of viral URI. High fever (>101-102°F) would raise concern for bacterial infection (sinusitis, pneumonia).

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the facial pressure and headaches.
historyQuestions.answer: It feels full under my eyes and between them. The headache gets worse when I bend forward or look down. It's more pressure than pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sinus pressure is common with viral URI due to mucosal inflammation. Bacterial sinusitis criteria: symptoms >10 days OR 'double worsening' OR severe symptoms (fever >102, purulent drainage) - not met here.

historyQuestions.text: Any shortness of breath or trouble breathing?
historyQuestions.answer: No, not really. I only get a little winded if I have a bad coughing fit, but otherwise breathing is fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No dyspnea argues against pneumonia. Important red flag to rule out.

historyQuestions.answer: My chest gets a little sore from coughing so much, but no sharp pain or anything when I breathe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chest wall soreness from coughing is common. Pleuritic pain (sharp, worse with breathing) would raise pneumonia concern.

historyQuestions.answer: The sore throat was only at the beginning and went away after a few days. Throat feels fine now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Resolved sore throat makes peritonsillar abscess very unlikely. Would expect severe, worsening throat pain.

historyQuestions.text: Any changes to your voice or difficulty swallowing?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a little hoarse from the postnasal drip, but no trouble swallowing. Nothing stuck in my throat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No 'hot potato' voice, no dysphagia, no drooling rules out serious pharyngeal/epiglottic pathology.

historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, my kid had the same thing last week. A couple coworkers too. There's definitely something going around.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sick contacts strongly support viral etiology - URIs are highly contagious.

historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, usually in the spring. But this feels different - I have the low fever and feel more run down than usual allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Patient can distinguish this from their usual allergies. Fever and sick contacts point to infection over allergies.

historyQuestions.answer: No, never have.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-smoker reduces concern for COPD exacerbation or complicated bronchitis.

social_exposure_settings.answer: My kid's in daycare. There's always something going around. I work in an open office with about 50 people.
social_exposure_settings.relevance: Daycare exposure is major source of viral transmission; open office increases workplace spread - classic URI epidemiology.

occupation_current_workplace.answer: Marketing firm. Open floor plan, lots of collaboration. People don't stay home when sick.
occupation_current_workplace.relevance: Open offices facilitate respiratory virus transmission; workplace culture affects presenteeism and spread.

housing_household_size.answer: Me, my spouse, and two kids - 4 and 6 years old. Small house, hard to isolate.
housing_household_size.relevance: Young children in household dramatically increases exposure to respiratory viruses; close quarters aids transmission.

social_preventive_behaviors.answer: I try to wash my hands but with the kids it's constant chaos. Got my flu shot though.
social_preventive_behaviors.relevance: Hand hygiene is key prevention; vaccination helps narrow differential - less likely influenza if vaccinated.

housing_air_quality_ventilation.answer: House is pretty well sealed. We keep the windows closed in winter.
housing_air_quality_ventilation.relevance: Poor ventilation can increase viral transmission in enclosed spaces during winter months.

social_sleep_patterns.answer: Not great with the kids. Maybe 6 hours a night, interrupted. I'm always tired.
social_sleep_patterns.relevance: Sleep deprivation impairs immune function and increases susceptibility to viral infections.

occupation_ability_to_rest.answer: Hard to take sick days. Deadline pressure. I usually push through.
occupation_ability_to_rest.relevance: Inability to rest prolongs illness and increases workplace transmission to others.

environmental_seasonal_factors.answer: It's winter. Everyone's cooped up inside.
environmental_seasonal_factors.relevance: Winter crowding indoors increases viral transmission - classic seasonality of URI.

physicalExam.finding: BP 116/74, HR 92, RR 18, Temp 99.3°F, SpO2 98% on RA. Low-grade fever, otherwise normal vitals.
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever typical of URI. Normal oxygen saturation argues against pneumonia.

physicalExam.finding: Alert, comfortable, no acute distress. Occasional cough during exam. Mild nasal congestion voice. Tissues in hand.
physicalExam.reasoning: Not toxic-appearing. Comfortable appearance argues against serious bacterial infection.

physicalExam.finding: Tympanic membranes intact bilaterally with slightly dull landmarks. No bulging, no erythema, no effusion. Mild eustachian tube dysfunction.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild eustachian tube dysfunction explains ear fullness. No otitis media.

physicalExam.finding: Nasal mucosa edematous and erythematous. Turbinates swollen. Clear to slightly cloudy mucoid discharge. No purulent drainage.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mucosal inflammation with clear/mucoid discharge typical of viral URI. Purulent drainage would raise bacterial sinusitis concern.

physicalExam.finding: Mild tenderness to palpation over maxillary sinuses bilaterally. No frontal sinus tenderness. No periorbital swelling or erythema.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild sinus tenderness common in viral URI. Severe tenderness with fever/purulent discharge would suggest bacterial.

physicalExam.finding: Mild posterior pharyngeal erythema. Cobblestoning from postnasal drip. Tonsils normal size, no exudates. Uvula midline. No trismus.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cobblestoning is classic for postnasal drip. No tonsillar asymmetry or uvula deviation rules out peritonsillar abscess.

physicalExam.finding: Small, mobile, non-tender anterior cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. No posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Neck supple.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mild reactive lymphadenopathy common with viral URI.

physicalExam.finding: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Good air movement. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi. No focal findings.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Clear lungs essentially rule out pneumonia. No focal findings to suggest consolidation.

physicalExam.finding: Resonant throughout all lung fields. No dullness.
physicalExam.reasoning: No dullness argues against consolidation or effusion.

labs.name: No labs indicated
labs.result: Clinical diagnosis - no labs necessary for uncomplicated viral URI.
labs.reasoning: Viral URI is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS. Labs not indicated unless concerned for bacterial infection or complications. Ordering labs is overutilization.

labs.name: Rapid strep test
labs.result: Not indicated - patient does not have significant pharyngitis currently.
labs.reasoning: Centor criteria: fever, tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical nodes, absence of cough. This patient doesn't meet criteria for strep testing.

labs.name: COVID-19 test
labs.result: Rapid antigen negative. Could consider if high community prevalence or patient concerned.
labs.reasoning: Reasonable to test if high community prevalence, immunocompromised, or for epidemiologic/isolation purposes.

labs.name: Influenza rapid test
labs.result: Not indicated - patient doesn't have classic flu symptoms (sudden onset, high fever, myalgias).
labs.reasoning: Could consider during flu season if would change management (antivirals). This patient's presentation is more URI than influenza.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated URI.
labs.reasoning: No indication for CBC. WBC count doesn't reliably distinguish viral from bacterial infection.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.result: Not indicated - clinical exam sufficient.
labs.reasoning: May help in lower respiratory infections but not helpful or cost-effective for obvious viral URI.

labs.name: C-Reactive Protein
labs.result: Not indicated - adds no useful information.
labs.reasoning: Non-specific marker. Doesn't distinguish viral from bacterial and wouldn't change management.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Not indicated with clear lung exam and no red flags.
imaging.reasoning: CXR NOT needed for URI with clear lungs, no hypoxia, no dyspnea, no high fever. Would be overutilization.

imaging.name: Sinus CT
imaging.result: Not indicated - does not meet criteria for bacterial sinusitis.
imaging.reasoning: Imaging NOT recommended for acute sinusitis. Clinical criteria not met for bacterial infection. Would be significant overutilization.

imaging.name: Sinus X-ray
imaging.reasoning: Sinus X-rays are obsolete. Even if obtained, don't change management.

procedures.name: No procedures indicated
procedures.result: No procedures needed for uncomplicated URI.
procedures.reasoning: URI is managed entirely with supportive care. No invasive procedures needed.

treatments.name: Supportive care counseling (rest, fluids, humidifier)
treatments.result: Counseled on supportive measures. Expect symptoms to improve over next 1-2 weeks.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE: Viral URI is self-limited. Rest, fluids, and time are the mainstays of treatment.

treatments.name: OTC symptom relief (decongestants, antihistamines, cough suppressants)
treatments.result: Can continue DayQuil/NyQuil. Pseudoephedrine helps congestion. First-gen antihistamine helps runny nose. Honey for cough.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic relief only. These medications help symptoms but don't shorten illness duration.

treatments.name: Nasal saline irrigation
treatments.result: Continue saline spray. Neti pot or sinus rinse can also help. Evidence-based for symptom relief.
treatments.reasoning: Saline irrigation is one of the few EVIDENCE-BASED treatments for URI and sinusitis symptoms.

treatments.name: Honey for cough
treatments.result: Honey 1-2 tsp at bedtime can help cough. Evidence shows it's as effective as OTC cough medicines.
treatments.reasoning: Honey has evidence for cough relief, especially nighttime cough. Do not use in children <1 year (botulism risk).

treatments.name: Zinc lozenges
treatments.result: May shorten cold duration if started within 24 hours of symptom onset.
treatments.reasoning: Some evidence for benefit if started very early. This patient is 7 days into illness, so unlikely to help now.

treatments.name: Vitamin C
treatments.result: No significant benefit for treatment of established cold.
treatments.reasoning: Prophylactic use may slightly reduce cold duration but doesn't help once sick.

treatments.name: Antibiotics
treatments.result: NOT indicated - this is a viral infection. Antibiotics will not help.
treatments.reasoning: ANTIBIOTICS DO NOT HELP VIRAL URI. Inappropriate prescribing contributes to resistance, causes side effects (C. diff, allergic reactions), and increases costs. Patient does NOT meet criteria for bacterial sinusitis.

treatments.name: Systemic steroids (prednisone)
treatments.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated URI.
treatments.reasoning: No role for systemic steroids in viral URI. Risk of immunosuppression without benefit.

treatments.name: Intranasal steroids (fluticasone)
treatments.result: May help with congestion if symptoms persist or for underlying allergic rhinitis.
treatments.reasoning: Limited evidence for acute viral URI, but reasonable if prolonged symptoms or allergic component.

treatments.name: Prescription cough suppressant (benzonatate, codeine)
treatments.result: Not indicated. OTC options and honey are sufficient.
treatments.reasoning: Prescription cough suppressants rarely needed for viral URI. Opioid cough suppressants carry risks.

consults.name: No consults needed
consults.reasoning: Viral URI is managed in primary care. No specialist consultation needed.

consults.name: ENT referral
consults.reasoning: Would consider ENT only for recurrent sinusitis, nasal polyps, or structural abnormalities.

disposition.name: Discharge with return precautions
disposition.result: Discharge home. Return if: fever >102°F, symptoms worsen after initial improvement ('double worsening'), shortness of breath, chest pain, symptoms >10-14 days without improvement.
disposition.reasoning: Appropriate discharge with clear return precautions. Educate on expected duration (7-14 days).

disposition.name: Scheduled follow-up
disposition.result: Not needed unless symptoms persist >10-14 days.
disposition.reasoning: Routine follow-up not necessary. PRN if no improvement or worsening.

actualDiagnosis.name: Viral Upper Respiratory Infection (Common Cold)
keyFindings: Classic URI presentation: gradual onset, nasal congestion, postnasal drip, cough, low-grade fever
7-day duration with plateau/early improvement - typical viral course
Clear lung exam - rules out pneumonia
No red flags: no high fever, no dyspnea, no hypoxia, no focal lung findings
Does NOT meet criteria for bacterial sinusitis (no high fever, no purulent drainage, no 'double worsening', <10 days)
teachingPoints: VIRAL URI IS CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: No labs or imaging needed for typical presentation. Save resources and avoid unnecessary radiation.
ANTIBIOTICS DON'T HELP VIRAL URI: Most URIs are viral. Antibiotics won't help and contribute to resistance. Resist patient pressure for antibiotics.
SPUTUM COLOR DOESN'T INDICATE BACTERIA: Yellow or even green sputum is common in viral infections and doesn't mean antibiotics are needed.

BACTERIAL SINUSITIS CRITERIA: Symptoms >10 days without improvement, OR 'double worsening' (improving then getting worse), OR severe symptoms (fever >102, purulent drainage for 3+ days). Most 'sinus infections' are viral.
KNOW THE RED FLAGS: High fever (>102), dyspnea, hypoxia, focal lung findings, severe sore throat with trismus, drooling, stridor - these need further workup.
TIME IS THE BEST TREATMENT: URI symptoms peak at day 3-4 and typically resolve by day 10-14. Set expectations.
PATIENT EDUCATION IS KEY: Explain why antibiotics aren't helpful. Discuss supportive care. Give clear return precautions.

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Guidelines for Antibiotic Use in Acute Respiratory Tract Infections 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. MMWR. 2016;65(1):1-5
keyPoints: Antibiotics NOT indicated for uncomplicated URI (common cold)
Viral URI is self-limited; supportive care is appropriate
Patient education is key to reducing inappropriate antibiotic requests
Sputum color does not reliably indicate bacterial infection
Set expectations: URI symptoms last 7-14 days

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Rhinosinusitis 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Chow AW et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2012;54(8):e72-e112
keyPoints: Most rhinosinusitis is viral - antibiotics not indicated
Bacterial sinusitis criteria: symptoms >10 days without improvement, OR double worsening, OR severe onset (fever ≥39°C + purulent drainage ≥3 days)
Imaging not recommended for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis
If antibiotics indicated: Amoxicillin-clavulanate first-line
Saline irrigation for symptomatic relief

closureScript.diagnosis: Good news - your lungs sound clear and this looks like a regular cold. I know you've been miserable with the cough and congestion, but your exam and symptoms tell me this is a viral infection that's actually already starting to get better.
closureScript.plan: For treatment, I recommend continuing what you're doing - rest, fluids, and the over-the-counter cold medicines you've been taking. Honey can help with the cough if you use it. Salt water gargles for throat irritation. A humidifier at night can help with congestion. Most importantly, this should keep improving over the next week.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop a high fever over 102, if your symptoms are getting significantly worse after they started getting better, if you have trouble breathing, chest pain, or if you're not improving after 10-14 days.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know it might seem like an antibiotic would help you feel better faster, but I want to explain why I'm not prescribing one today. Antibiotics only work on bacteria, and colds are caused by viruses. If I gave you an antibiotic, it wouldn't help your cold at all, but it could cause side effects like stomach upset or diarrhea, and it contributes to antibiotic resistance. I promise I'm not withholding something that would help - I'm actually protecting you from unnecessary medication. If you're not better in 10 days, we can reassess.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if symptoms worsened after initial improvement?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient was getting better, then fever and facial pain returned worse

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this cough and stuffy nose for about a week
chiefComplaint.modified: I was getting better, but 2 days ago I got worse again - now I have facial pain and thick green discharge

feedback.correct: 'Double worsening' (improved then worsened) meets criteria for acute bacterial sinusitis. NOW antibiotics are indicated - amoxicillin-clavulanate first line. Also indicated if symptoms >10 days or severe onset (fever >102 + purulent discharge).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of bacterial criteria. What defines 'double worsening'?
feedback.incorrect: Bacterial sinusitis criteria: 1) Symptoms >10 days without improvement, OR 2) 'Double worsening' (improving then getting worse), OR 3) Severe onset (fever >102 + purulent discharge for 3+ days). This patient has #2 - antibiotics now appropriate.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral URI = self-limited, no antibiotics. Bacterial sinusitis = specific criteria must be met before antibiotics are indicated. Most 'sinus infections' are viral - look for specific criteria.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the cough lasted 3 weeks with paroxysms?
whatIfScenarios.description: Persistent severe coughing fits, post-tussive vomiting, no fever

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this cough and stuffy nose for about a week
chiefComplaint.modified: I've had coughing fits for 3 weeks. I cough so hard I vomit sometimes. I can't stop once I start.

feedback.correct: Paroxysmal cough + post-tussive vomiting + duration >2 weeks = think pertussis. Confirm with PCR. Treat with azithromycin (reduces transmission even if doesn't shorten course). Contact precautions. Notify public health.
feedback.partial: Good suspicion for pertussis. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Pertussis: paroxysmal coughing fits, post-tussive vomiting, inspiratory 'whoop' (adults often lack whoop), afebrile, lasts 6-10 weeks. PCR for diagnosis. Azithromycin x 5 days - mainly reduces transmission. Highly contagious - protect infants and unvaccinated.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral URI = improves by 10-14 days. Pertussis = paroxysmal cough lasting weeks, no fever, post-tussive vomiting. The duration and character of cough are key.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were focal lung findings and hypoxia?
whatIfScenarios.description: Cough with fever 102.8°F, focal crackles, SpO2 92%

chiefComplaint.original: I've had this cough and stuffy nose for about a week
chiefComplaint.modified: I've had cough and fever for 4 days, now I'm short of breath

feedback.correct: High fever + focal crackles + hypoxia = pneumonia, not URI. CXR to confirm. Start appropriate antibiotics. Assess severity with CURB-65 or PSI for disposition.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of pneumonia. What distinguishes it from URI?
feedback.incorrect: Red flags that this isn't simple URI: high fever (>102), focal lung findings (crackles/consolidation), hypoxia, significant dyspnea. This needs CXR and antibiotics - CAP treatment, not URI symptomatic care.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: URI = clear lungs, low-grade fever, no hypoxia, self-limited. Pneumonia = focal findings, high fever, hypoxia, needs antibiotics. Lung exam and vital signs differentiate.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: AAO Retinal Artery Occlusion Guidelines 2020
keyPoints: Ocular emergency - immediate referral
Ocular massage and IOP lowering
Rule out GCA with ESR/CRP

female-72-crao.name: Emily Martinez
female-72-crao.chiefComplaint: I suddenly can't see out of my right eye
female-72-crao.variantDescription: Central retinal artery occlusion with GCA

missedConsequences.Central Retinal Artery Occlusion: Permanent vision loss within 90-120 minutes. 'Stroke of the eye.'
missedConsequences.Giant Cell Arteritis: Untreated GCA causes bilateral blindness and stroke.

commonPitfalls: Not recognizing CRAO as emergency with narrow treatment window
Failing to ask about GCA symptoms (jaw claudication, headache)
Not checking ESR/CRP urgently for GCA evaluation

allDiagnoses.name: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden painless monocular vision loss in elderly with CV risk factors.

allDiagnoses.name: Giant Cell Arteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause CRAO. Age >50, headache, jaw claudication. Needs emergent steroids.

allDiagnoses.name: Retinal Detachment
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause sudden vision loss. Look for flashes, floaters, curtain.

allDiagnoses.name: Stroke (CVA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Posterior circulation stroke causes vision loss. Check for other neuro deficits.

female-72-crao.oldcartsNarrative: "About 2 hours ago, I was reading and suddenly everything went dark in my right eye. Like a shade came down. No pain. I've had headaches at my temples for weeks and my jaw gets tired when I chew."

historyQuestions.text: How quickly did the vision loss happen?
historyQuestions.answer: Instantly. One second I could see, the next - nothing. Like a light switch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sudden, painless, complete monocular vision loss = arterial occlusion.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the jaw tiredness.
historyQuestions.answer: When I chew, especially steak, my jaw muscles get tired. I have to stop and rest.
historyQuestions.reasoning: JAW CLAUDICATION is highly specific for GCA.

historyQuestions.answer: Started 3 weeks ago. Mostly at my temples. Different from any headache I've had.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New-onset temporal headache in elderly = GCA red flag.

historyQuestions.answer: A few times things went gray in that eye for minutes then came back.
historyQuestions.reasoning: AMAUROSIS FUGAX is TIA equivalent - warning sign before complete occlusion.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_cv_risk.answer: High blood pressure 20 years, high cholesterol. Miss doses sometimes.
social_cv_risk.relevance: CV risk factors predispose to retinal artery occlusion.

physicalExam.finding: Right eye: pale retina, CHERRY-RED SPOT at macula, attenuated arteries. Left normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: PATHOGNOMONIC: cherry-red spot with pale ischemic retina.

physicalExam.finding: Right temporal artery: tender, thickened, nodular. Diminished pulse.
physicalExam.reasoning: Abnormal temporal artery highly suggests GCA.

labs.name: ESR (Westergren)
labs.result: ESR 78 mm/hr (markedly elevated, normal <20 for age). Strongly supports GCA.
labs.reasoning: ESR is the most important lab for GCA. ESR >50 in elderly with vision loss or headache = start steroids immediately. Don't wait for biopsy.

labs.name: CRP
labs.result: CRP 45 mg/L (elevated, normal <3). Elevated inflammatory markers.
labs.reasoning: CRP adds to inflammatory marker profile. Combined with ESR increases specificity for GCA.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 8.4, Hgb 10.8 (mild anemia of chronic inflammation), Plt 412 (thrombocytosis).
labs.reasoning: Normocytic anemia and thrombocytosis often seen in GCA - inflammatory state. Supports diagnosis.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.2, Cr 1.0, Glucose 118. Baseline before starting high-dose steroids.
labs.reasoning: Baseline before steroids. Monitor glucose and electrolytes on prednisone.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.result: AST 28, ALT 32, Alk phos 145 (mildly elevated). Mild elevation seen in GCA.
labs.reasoning: Elevated alkaline phosphatase is common in GCA. Also baseline for steroid monitoring.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.result: INR 1.0, PTT 28. Normal coagulation.
labs.reasoning: Check before temporal artery biopsy.

imaging.name: Dilated Fundoscopy (documented)
imaging.result: Right eye: Cherry-red spot at macula, diffuse retinal pallor/edema, arteriolar attenuation. Left eye: Normal.
imaging.reasoning: PATHOGNOMONIC finding for CRAO. Cherry-red spot is choroidal perfusion visible through thin fovea against backdrop of ischemic/pale retina.

imaging.name: Temporal Artery Ultrasound
imaging.result: Right temporal artery: Halo sign present. Wall thickening. Suggestive of GCA.
imaging.reasoning: Non-invasive test for GCA. Halo sign (dark hypoechoic ring around artery) has 90%+ specificity. Supports diagnosis while awaiting biopsy.

imaging.name: CTA Head/Neck
imaging.result: Right ICA 60-70% stenosis at bifurcation. Intracranial vessels patent. No aneurysm.
imaging.reasoning: Evaluates for embolic source. Carotid stenosis can cause CRAO without GCA - but this patient has GCA symptoms.

imaging.name: MRI Brain with DWI
imaging.result: No acute infarct. No posterior circulation stroke. Optic nerve signal normal.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out posterior circulation stroke in patient with sudden vision loss. Important differential.

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.result: No thrombus, no vegetation, no PFO. EF 60%.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out cardiac embolic source for CRAO.

procedures.name: Temporal Artery Biopsy
procedures.result: Scheduled for tomorrow (within 2 weeks of steroid start). Will obtain 2-3 cm segment.
procedures.reasoning: Gold standard for GCA diagnosis. Should be at least 2 cm to avoid skip lesions. Do NOT delay steroids waiting for biopsy - biopsy positive up to 2-4 weeks after steroid start.

procedures.name: Ocular Massage (time-sensitive)
procedures.result: Attempted intermittent firm pressure over closed eye for 10 seconds on, 10 seconds off. No improvement.
procedures.reasoning: Traditional maneuver to dislodge embolus. Low evidence but benign. Only effective within first few hours of symptom onset.

procedures.name: Anterior Chamber Paracentesis (if <4 hours)
procedures.result: Not performed - symptom onset >4 hours ago.
procedures.reasoning: May reduce IOP and allow retinal reperfusion. Only within 4 hours of onset. Ophthalmology procedure.

treatments.name: IV Methylprednisolone 1g daily x 3 days
treatments.result: Started immediately upon suspicion of GCA. Do NOT wait for biopsy.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL - start high-dose IV steroids IMMEDIATELY if GCA suspected. Prevents contralateral eye involvement. Biopsy can be done within 2 weeks - don't wait.

treatments.name: Prednisone 60mg daily after IV course
treatments.result: Will transition to oral prednisone 60mg daily (1mg/kg) after IV pulse. Slow taper over months.
treatments.reasoning: Long-term steroids required. Typical course is 1-2 years. Taper based on symptoms and ESR/CRP.

treatments.name: Proton Pump Inhibitor
treatments.result: Omeprazole 40mg daily for GI prophylaxis on high-dose steroids.
treatments.reasoning: GI prophylaxis mandatory on high-dose steroids. Reduces ulcer risk.

treatments.name: Calcium + Vitamin D
treatments.result: Calcium 1200mg + Vitamin D 1000 IU daily for bone protection.
treatments.reasoning: Osteoporosis prophylaxis on long-term steroids. DEXA at baseline.

treatments.name: Bisphosphonate (if indicated)
treatments.result: Alendronate 70mg weekly if expected steroid course >3 months.
treatments.reasoning: Consider bisphosphonate for bone protection on long-term steroids, especially postmenopausal women.

treatments.name: Low-dose Aspirin
treatments.result: Aspirin 81mg daily added.
treatments.reasoning: Studies suggest aspirin may reduce ischemic events in GCA. Often added to steroid therapy.

consults.name: Ophthalmology (EMERGENT)
consults.result: Ophthalmology consulted emergently. Confirmed CRAO with APD. Agrees with immediate steroids for GCA.
consults.reasoning: Emergent ophthalmology evaluation for any acute vision loss. Confirms CRAO diagnosis. May attempt additional interventions.

consults.name: Rheumatology
consults.result: Rheumatology consulted for GCA management. Will co-manage steroid taper and monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Long-term GCA management requires rheumatology. Steroid-sparing agents (tocilizumab) may be considered.

consults.name: Vascular Surgery (for biopsy)
consults.result: Vascular surgery scheduled for temporal artery biopsy tomorrow.
consults.reasoning: Arrange temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks of steroid start. Longer segment (2-3 cm) preferred.

disposition.name: Admit for IV Steroids and Workup
disposition.result: Admitted for IV methylprednisolone, temporal artery biopsy, and contralateral eye monitoring.
disposition.reasoning: Admission for IV steroids, close monitoring, and coordination of biopsy. Contralateral eye at high risk without immediate treatment.

disposition.name: Low Vision Rehabilitation Referral
disposition.result: Low vision services consulted for right eye permanent vision loss.
disposition.reasoning: CRAO vision loss is usually permanent. Patient needs rehabilitation and adaptive services.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were flashing lights and floaters?
whatIfScenarios.description: Acute vision loss preceded by photopsias (flashing lights) and new floaters, curtain over vision

chiefComplaint.original: I suddenly can't see out of my right eye
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been seeing flashing lights and lots of new floaters, now there's like a curtain coming over my vision from the side

feedback.correct: Flashing lights + sudden floaters + progressive peripheral vision loss = RETINAL DETACHMENT until proven otherwise. Emergent ophthalmology - macula-on detachments need surgery within 24-48 hours. Macula-off is less urgent but still requires surgery.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of retinal detachment. What's the urgency?
feedback.incorrect: Retinal detachment warning signs: photopsias (flashing lights), sudden shower of floaters, peripheral vision loss progressing centrally ('curtain' effect). EMERGENT ophthalmology consult. Macula-on = surgical emergency within 24 hours. Macula-off = less urgent but still needs surgery.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: CRAO = sudden COMPLETE central vision loss, no warning signs, cherry-red spot. Retinal detachment = progressive peripheral loss, photopsias, floaters. The prodrome and pattern of vision loss distinguish them.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was painful eye movement in a young woman?
whatIfScenarios.description: Subacute vision loss with pain on eye movement in a 32-year-old woman

chiefComplaint.original: I suddenly can't see out of my right eye
chiefComplaint.modified: 32-year-old woman: My vision has gotten blurry over the past few days and it hurts to move my eye

feedback.correct: Optic neuritis: subacute vision loss + pain with eye movement + APD. Often first presentation of MS (especially in young women). MRI brain with and without contrast, LP may be considered. IV steroids speed recovery but don't change final outcome. Ophthalmology and neurology.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of optic neuritis. What workup is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Optic neuritis: subacute central vision loss, pain with EYE MOVEMENT (not just pain), APD, color desaturation (red looks 'washed out'). Often first MS presentation. MRI brain (look for demyelinating lesions). IV steroids hasten recovery. Neurology referral for MS workup.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: CRAO = sudden, PAINLESS, older patient, vascular risk factors. Optic neuritis = subacute, PAINFUL eye movement, young woman, think MS. Age, timing, and pain distinguish them.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were no GCA symptoms?
whatIfScenarios.description: CRAO in patient with atrial fibrillation, no headache or jaw claudication

chiefComplaint.original: I suddenly can't see out of my right eye
chiefComplaint.modified: Sudden vision loss in right eye. I have atrial fibrillation. No headache or jaw problems.

feedback.correct: CRAO without GCA symptoms = embolic until proven otherwise. Same urgency as TIA/stroke workup: carotid imaging, echo (looking for thrombus), anticoagulation if AFib. Still check ESR/CRP to rule out GCA in anyone >50.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of embolic source. What workup is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Embolic CRAO: treat as 'eye stroke.' Full stroke workup: carotid US/CTA, echo (TEE if needed), Holter if no known afib. If afib present, ensure anticoagulation. Still check ESR/CRP in anyone >50 to rule out GCA even without symptoms.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: CRAO with GCA symptoms = steroids immediately, temporal artery biopsy. CRAO without GCA symptoms = stroke workup, cardiac/carotid source. Both need emergent ophthalmology and both have poor visual prognosis.

physicalExam.reasoning: MUST CHECK. Normal acuity is reassuring.

physicalExam.finding: Right: diffuse injection, watery discharge, follicles on lower tarsal conjunctiva. Left: clear.
physicalExam.reasoning: Follicles and watery discharge = viral conjunctivitis.

physicalExam.finding: Pupils equal, round, reactive. No APD.

labs.name: No laboratory testing indicated
labs.result: Viral conjunctivitis is a clinical diagnosis based on history and examination findings
labs.reasoning: Clinical diagnosis. Labs do not confirm or change management of typical viral conjunctivitis.

labs.name: Conjunctival culture
labs.result: Not indicated for typical viral presentation
labs.reasoning: Reserve for: severe purulent discharge, suspected gonococcal/chlamydial conjunctivitis, contact lens-related keratitis, neonates, immunocompromised. Not needed here.

labs.name: Adenoviral PCR testing
labs.result: Available but not routinely needed
labs.reasoning: Point-of-care adenoviral testing exists but doesn't change management. Only useful for epidemiological purposes or to confirm diagnosis for infection control.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.reasoning: Conjunctivitis is diagnosed clinically. No role for imaging.

imaging.name: Slit lamp examination (if available)
imaging.result: Can be performed to better visualize structures and rule out corneal involvement
imaging.reasoning: Slit lamp allows detailed examination of cornea for infiltrates, anterior chamber for cells/flare. Not strictly necessary for classic viral conjunctivitis with normal exam.

procedures.name: Fluorescein staining
procedures.result: No corneal staining - no epithelial defect
procedures.reasoning: Rules out corneal abrasion or ulcer. Perform if any concern for corneal involvement, contact lens use, or trauma history.

procedures.name: Intraocular pressure measurement
procedures.reasoning: Normal IOP rules out glaucoma. Check if any concern for angle closure (pain, decreased vision, halos). May not be necessary for classic viral conjunctivitis without pain.

treatments.name: Supportive care - cold compresses and artificial tears
treatments.result: Cold compresses several times daily for comfort. Artificial tears PRN for irritation (preservative-free preferred).
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment. Viral conjunctivitis is self-limited. Supportive care only. Cold compresses reduce discomfort.

treatments.name: NO antibiotic drops - viral etiology
treatments.result: Antibiotic eye drops are NOT indicated - this is viral
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics do not treat viral infections. Inappropriate antibiotic use promotes resistance and adds cost without benefit.

treatments.name: Hand hygiene and infection control counseling
treatments.result: Extensive counseling on hand washing, avoid touching eyes, don't share towels/pillows, discard eye makeup, stay home until discharge resolves.
treatments.reasoning: Adenoviral conjunctivitis is HIGHLY CONTAGIOUS. Infection control is critical to prevent spread to household contacts and community.

treatments.name: Contact lens precautions
treatments.result: N/A - patient doesn't wear contacts. If wore contacts: discontinue until fully resolved, discard current lenses and case.
treatments.reasoning: Contact lens wearers must stop wearing lenses during infection. Discard potentially contaminated lenses and cases.

treatments.name: Topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer
treatments.result: Not indicated for viral - would be for allergic conjunctivitis
treatments.reasoning: Antihistamine drops are for allergic conjunctivitis (itching predominant). Not helpful for viral.

treatments.name: Topical NSAID (ketorolac)
treatments.result: Can consider for symptomatic relief of discomfort
treatments.reasoning: May reduce discomfort. Not first-line but can help if significant irritation. Expensive.

treatments.name: Topical corticosteroid
treatments.result: NOT for routine use - can worsen herpes, cause glaucoma
treatments.reasoning: AVOID in primary care. Steroids can worsen HSV keratitis (geographic ulcer), cause glaucoma, and delay healing. Only used by ophthalmology for specific indications.

consults.name: No specialty consultation needed for typical case
consults.result: Viral conjunctivitis is managed in primary care
consults.reasoning: Straightforward viral conjunctivitis doesn't need ophthalmology referral.

consults.name: Ophthalmology referral
consults.result: NOT indicated for typical viral conjunctivitis
consults.reasoning: Refer ONLY if: contact lens wearer with suspected keratitis, severe purulent discharge (gonococcal), vision changes, corneal involvement, no improvement >10-14 days, suspected herpes (vesicles, dendrites).

disposition.name: Discharge with supportive care
disposition.result: Discharge with artificial tears, cold compresses, hygiene instructions. Self-limited - resolves 7-14 days. May spread to other eye. Return if: pain, vision changes, photophobia, worsening after 7 days.
disposition.reasoning: Viral conjunctivitis is managed outpatient. Clear return precautions for warning signs of more serious pathology.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was copious purulent discharge?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe conjunctivitis with thick greenish-yellow pus, lid swelling, and high-risk sexual history

chiefComplaint.original: My eye is red and watery
chiefComplaint.modified: My eye is swollen shut with thick yellow-green pus pouring out. I'm sexually active with multiple partners.

feedback.correct: Hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis = GONOCOCCAL until proven otherwise. EMERGENCY - can perforate cornea in 24-48 hours. Gram stain/culture, systemic ceftriaxone (not topical only), treat for chlamydia too, ophthalmology STAT, contact tracing.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of GC conjunctivitis. Why is this an emergency?
feedback.incorrect: Gonococcal conjunctivitis: hyperacute, copious purulent discharge, rapid lid swelling. CAN PERFORATE CORNEA in 24-48 hours. Treatment: ceftriaxone IM + azithromycin (chlamydia coinfection). Ophthalmology emergent. Screen/treat sexual partners.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral = watery, mild, self-limited. Bacterial (routine) = mucopurulent, topical antibiotics adequate. Gonococcal = HYPERpurulent, can perforate, needs SYSTEMIC treatment. The amount and character of discharge matters.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were vesicles on the eyelid?
whatIfScenarios.description: Red eye with vesicular lesions on eyelid and dendritic lesion on cornea

chiefComplaint.original: My eye is red and watery
chiefComplaint.modified: My eye is red and I have little blisters on my eyelid. My vision seems a bit blurry.

feedback.correct: Vesicles + red eye = HERPES until proven otherwise. Examine cornea with fluorescein - dendritic ulcer is pathognomonic. Treatment: topical antiviral (ganciclovir gel, trifluridine) or oral valacyclovir. NO STEROIDS - will worsen dramatically. Ophthalmology referral.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of HSV involvement. What should you NOT give?
feedback.incorrect: HSV keratitis: vesicular lesions on lid/periorbital, dendritic or geographic corneal ulcer on fluorescein. NEVER give steroids (dramatic worsening). Treatment: topical or oral antivirals. Can cause recurrent disease and scarring. Ophthalmology referral.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Viral conjunctivitis = watery, no vesicles, no corneal involvement, self-limited. HSV keratitis = vesicles, dendritic ulcer, needs antivirals, can scar cornea. Look for vesicles and stain the cornea.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a contact lens wearer?
whatIfScenarios.description: Red eye in contact lens wearer with pain and photophobia

chiefComplaint.original: My eye is red and watery
chiefComplaint.modified: I wear contacts and my eye is red and painful. Light really bothers me.

feedback.correct: Contact lens wearer + red eye + pain/photophobia = KERATITIS until proven otherwise. Remove lens immediately, examine cornea with fluorescein, start topical fluoroquinolone. Pseudomonas can perforate rapidly. Ophthalmology same-day.
feedback.partial: Good concern for keratitis. What organism is most concerning?
feedback.incorrect: Contact lens wearers with red eye: assume KERATITIS, not conjunctivitis. Pseudomonas is common and aggressive - can perforate in hours. Remove lens, stain cornea, start topical fluoroquinolone q1h, ophthalmology urgently. Acanthamoeba also possible (pain out of proportion).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-contact lens wearer red eye = often conjunctivitis. Contact lens wearer + pain/photophobia = keratitis until proven otherwise. Contact lens use changes the urgency and differential entirely.

physicalExam.finding: Right: ciliary flush (injection around limbus), hazy cornea. Left: clear.

physicalExam.finding: Right eye feels ROCK-HARD. Left feels normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated IOP makes globe feel hard - classic finding.

labs.name: Labs should not delay treatment
labs.result: This is a clinical emergency - do not delay treatment to obtain laboratory studies
labs.reasoning: Acute angle closure is a CLINICAL EMERGENCY. Treatment must start immediately based on clinical findings. Labs do not change acute management.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: May check baseline renal function before systemic therapy, but do not delay treatment
labs.reasoning: Acetazolamide and mannitol have renal considerations. Can check while initiating treatment, not before.

imaging.name: Tonometry (Intraocular Pressure measurement)
imaging.result: Right eye IOP: 58 mmHg (CRITICAL - normal <21). Left eye: 16 mmHg (normal). Confirms diagnosis.
imaging.reasoning: CONFIRMS DIAGNOSIS. IOP typically 40-80 mmHg in acute angle closure. Perform immediately if available. Do not delay treatment while awaiting tonometry if clinical picture is classic.

imaging.name: Gonioscopy
imaging.result: To be performed by ophthalmology - will confirm closed angle
imaging.reasoning: Definitive test to visualize iridocorneal angle. Performed by ophthalmology. Shows closed angle on affected side.

imaging.name: Slit lamp examination
imaging.result: Corneal edema with microcystic changes. Shallow anterior chamber. Mid-dilated pupil. Cells and flare.
imaging.reasoning: Shows corneal edema, shallow anterior chamber, and inflammatory changes. Performed by ophthalmology or experienced provider.

treatments.name: Timolol 0.5% (beta-blocker eye drop)
treatments.result: Timolol 0.5% 1 drop to affected eye STAT - decreases aqueous production
treatments.reasoning: First-line IOP-lowering drop. Reduces aqueous humor production. Avoid in asthma/COPD, heart block, severe bradycardia.

treatments.name: Brimonidine 0.2% (alpha-agonist eye drop)
treatments.result: Brimonidine 0.2% 1 drop to affected eye - reduces aqueous production
treatments.reasoning: Alpha-2 agonist decreases aqueous production. Can use with timolol for additive effect.

treatments.name: Pilocarpine 1-2% (miotic eye drop)
treatments.result: Pilocarpine 2% every 15 minutes x 2-3 doses - constricts pupil to open angle
treatments.reasoning: Miotic constricts pupil, pulling iris away from trabecular meshwork. May not work initially if IOP >40 (ischemic iris). Give after IOP starts to drop.

treatments.name: Acetazolamide (Diamox) IV or PO
treatments.result: CAUTION: Patient has SULFA ALLERGY - use with caution or choose alternative. If tolerated: Acetazolamide 500mg IV or 500mg PO.
treatments.reasoning: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor - decreases aqueous production systemically. SULFONAMIDE - this patient has sulfa allergy! May need alternative (mannitol, or ophthalmology guidance).

treatments.name: Mannitol IV (osmotic diuretic)
treatments.result: Mannitol 1-2 g/kg IV over 30-60 minutes if not responding to drops and acetazolamide
treatments.reasoning: Osmotic agent rapidly lowers IOP by drawing fluid from vitreous. Use if IOP remains elevated despite topical therapy. Alternative if sulfa allergy prevents acetazolamide.

treatments.name: Anti-emetic for nausea/vomiting
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for severe nausea
treatments.reasoning: AACG commonly causes severe nausea and vomiting (vagal response to pain and high IOP). Symptomatic relief while treating underlying cause.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: IV analgesic if severe pain - will improve as IOP decreases
treatments.reasoning: Severe pain is common. Treat with analgesics. Pain will improve as IOP is lowered.

treatments.name: Position patient supine
treatments.result: Position patient supine to help angle open with gravity
treatments.reasoning: Supine position may help move lens posteriorly and iris away from angle.

consults.name: Ophthalmology - EMERGENT
consults.result: EMERGENT ophthalmology consultation for laser iridotomy. Called immediately.
consults.reasoning: EMERGENT ophthalmology required for definitive treatment. Laser peripheral iridotomy creates alternative aqueous outflow path. Usually performed within hours of presentation.

consults.name: Prophylactic treatment of fellow eye
consults.result: Ophthalmology will also perform prophylactic laser iridotomy on unaffected eye
consults.reasoning: Fellow eye has similar anatomy and ~50% chance of developing acute angle closure. Prophylactic iridotomy prevents future attacks.

disposition.name: Admit or observe for urgent ophthalmology intervention
disposition.result: Patient requires urgent/emergent ophthalmology intervention. Admit or observe until laser iridotomy performed.
disposition.reasoning: Do NOT discharge. Patient needs laser iridotomy within hours. Permanent vision loss can occur if IOP remains elevated.

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.result: NEVER appropriate - this is an ophthalmic emergency
disposition.reasoning: NEVER discharge untreated AACG. Permanent vision loss within hours without treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Angle Closure Glaucoma
teachingPoints: PAINFUL red eye with DECREASED VISION = emergency until proven otherwise
Classic AACG: severe pain, nausea, halos, mid-dilated fixed pupil, rock-hard globe
Precipitants: dark environment, anticholinergic medications, sympathomimetics
This is EMERGENT - permanent vision loss within hours without treatment
Treatment: IOP-lowering drops (timolol, brimonidine, pilocarpine), acetazolamide, mannitol, ophthalmology STAT

guidelineReferences.name: AAO Preferred Practice Pattern: Primary Angle Closure
keyPoints: Acute angle closure is an ophthalmic emergency requiring immediate treatment
First-line: topical IOP-lowering agents (beta-blocker, alpha-agonist, prostaglandin analog)
Systemic agents: acetazolamide or mannitol if IOP remains elevated
Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is definitive treatment
Prophylactic LPI in fellow eye (50% develop AACG within 5 years)

guidelineReferences.name: EGS Guidelines: Primary Angle Closure
keyPoints: Immediate goal: IOP reduction to prevent optic nerve damage
Pilocarpine may be ineffective initially if iris ischemic from high IOP
Corneal indentation (gentle pressure on peripheral cornea) can break attack
Consider anterior chamber paracentesis if medical therapy fails
All AACG patients need gonioscopy of fellow eye and prophylactic treatment

closureScript.diagnosis: You have acute angle closure glaucoma - the pressure inside your eye is dangerously high because the drainage system is blocked. This is an emergency.
closureScript.plan: I'm starting medications right now to lower the pressure and calling ophthalmology for emergency evaluation. You'll likely need a laser procedure tonight to prevent permanent damage.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This is a sight-threatening emergency. Without treatment, you can permanently lose vision in this eye within hours.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know you're in terrible pain. The medications should start helping the pain as they lower the pressure. We're treating this as the emergency it is.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the IOP was elevated but patient asymptomatic?
whatIfScenarios.description: Routine eye exam finds IOP 28, patient has no symptoms, normal-appearing optic nerve

chiefComplaint.original: Terrible pain in my right eye and I can't see well
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm here for a routine eye exam. No complaints.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Ocular hypertension or early open-angle glaucoma

feedback.correct: Asymptomatic elevated IOP = chronic process (ocular hypertension or open-angle glaucoma). NOT an emergency. Needs ophthalmology follow-up for visual field testing, optic nerve evaluation, and possible treatment with IOP-lowering drops.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of chronic presentation. What distinguishes this from acute?
feedback.incorrect: Open-angle glaucoma is CHRONIC and ASYMPTOMATIC until late stages. Elevated IOP with open angles, gradual optic nerve damage, visual field loss starting peripherally. NOT an emergency - but needs ophthalmology referral for treatment to prevent progression.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Acute angle closure = PAINFUL, sudden vision loss, nausea, fixed pupil, emergency. Open-angle = asymptomatic, gradual, chronic management. The symptoms (or lack thereof) determine urgency.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was photophobia and a small pupil?
whatIfScenarios.description: Red painful eye with photophobia, miotic (small) pupil, and cells/flare in anterior chamber

chiefComplaint.original: Terrible pain in my right eye and I can't see well
chiefComplaint.modified: My eye is red, painful, and light really bothers it. The pupil looks smaller than the other eye.

feedback.correct: Photophobia + SMALL pupil = uveitis, NOT glaucoma. Glaucoma has DILATED fixed pupil. Uveitis needs slit lamp (cells/flare), topical steroids, cycloplegics. Consider systemic workup if recurrent (HLA-B27, sarcoid, etc).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of uveitis. What's the key pupil difference?
feedback.incorrect: Anterior uveitis (iritis): photophobia (including consensual), SMALL/reactive pupil (from inflammation), ciliary flush, cells/flare in anterior chamber. Treatment: topical steroids + cycloplegics. If recurrent, workup for systemic inflammatory disease.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: AACG = dilated FIXED pupil, no photophobia, rock-hard globe. Uveitis = SMALL reactive pupil, photophobia, cells/flare. Pupil size and reaction are key distinguishing features.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was a corneal ulcer visible?
whatIfScenarios.description: Red painful eye with visible white opacity on cornea in contact lens wearer

chiefComplaint.original: Terrible pain in my right eye and I can't see well
chiefComplaint.modified: My eye is red and painful. I wear contacts and slept in them. There's a white spot on my eye.

feedback.correct: White corneal opacity + contact lens use = BACTERIAL KERATITIS. Pseudomonas common in contact lens wearers. URGENT - can perforate in hours. Topical fluoroquinolone q1h, remove contact lens, ophthalmology same day, cultures before treatment if possible.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of keratitis. What's the urgency level?
feedback.incorrect: Bacterial keratitis in contact lens wearers: Pseudomonas is common and aggressive. White corneal infiltrate/ulcer, mucopurulent discharge. Can perforate within hours. Treatment: aggressive topical antibiotics (fluoroquinolone q1h), remove contact lens, urgent ophthalmology.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: AACG = clear cornea (or mildly hazy), no focal white lesion. Keratitis = visible white corneal infiltrate/ulcer. Looking at the cornea distinguishes these.

male-48-nihl.name: Ryan Mitchell
male-48-nihl.chiefComplaint: My wife says I never hear her anymore. I've been cranking up the TV volume too
male-48-nihl.variantDescription: Bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss from construction noise

missedConsequences.Noise-Induced Hearing Loss: Continued exposure without protection causes irreversible progression. Early intervention preserves remaining hearing.
missedConsequences.Acoustic Neuroma: Missing acoustic neuroma delays treatment of tumor.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about occupational and recreational noise exposure in hearing loss patients
Assuming hearing loss is just 'age-related' without considering noise history
Not emphasizing hearing protection to prevent further damage
Missing the classic '4kHz notch' pattern on audiogram
Failing to consider workers' compensation claim

allDiagnoses.name: Noise-Induced Hearing Loss
allDiagnoses.rationale: Construction worker + bilateral symmetric high-frequency loss + tinnitus + inadequate hearing protection = NIHL.

allDiagnoses.name: Presbycusis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Age-related loss is possible but 48 is young. Occupational exposure more likely cause.

allDiagnoses.name: Acoustic Neuroma
allDiagnoses.rationale: If asymmetric, must rule out - but this case is bilateral and symmetric.

allDiagnoses.name: Ototoxicity
allDiagnoses.rationale: No history of ototoxic medications.

male-48-nihl.oldcartsNarrative: "My hearing's been getting worse for years. My wife is always yelling at me to turn down the TV. I ask people to repeat themselves all the time. Also have this constant ringing - been there so long I almost don't notice it anymore. I've been in construction 25 years - jackhammers, saws, heavy equipment. Never really wore hearing protection much."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your noise exposure at work
historyQuestions.answer: 25 years in construction. Jackhammers, circular saws, heavy equipment, nail guns. It's loud all day, every day. Eight, ten hours at a time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CHRONIC HIGH-LEVEL noise exposure is the cause. Construction is one of the noisiest occupations.

historyQuestions.answer: We're supposed to wear it but honestly, most of us don't. It's uncomfortable, makes it hard to hear guys calling out, and it's just easier not to bother.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inadequate hearing protection despite exposure

historyQuestions.text: How has your hearing changed over time?
historyQuestions.answer: Gradual. Started noticing maybe 10 years ago. First I thought people were mumbling. Then I realized I wasn't hearing high-pitched sounds - like my granddaughter's voice is hard to hear.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual progression, high-frequency loss first - classic NIHL pattern

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the ringing in your ears
historyQuestions.answer: Both ears, constant high-pitched ringing. Had it for years. Never goes away. Worse at night when it's quiet.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilateral tinnitus is hallmark of NIHL

historyQuestions.answer: I go to the shooting range sometimes. Don't always wear ear protection there either. Used to go to a lot of rock concerts when I was younger.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Recreational noise adds to occupational exposure

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupational_noise.answer: Construction work for 25 years: jackhammers, saws, heavy equipment, nail guns. Continuous loud noise exposure 8-10 hours/day.
occupational_noise.relevance: OCCUPATIONAL NOISE is the exposure. Construction generates 85-120 dB - above safe limits. OSHA permissible limit is 90 dB for 8 hours.

occupational_ppe.answer: Rarely wears hearing protection. Says it's uncomfortable and impractical.
occupational_ppe.relevance: Without protection, cumulative noise damage is inevitable. Even 85 dB damages hearing over time.

hobbies_sports.relevance: Recreational noise exposures compound occupational damage.

physicalExam.finding: External canals clear bilaterally. Tympanic membranes normal, no perforation, no effusion.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal ear exam rules out conductive causes

physicalExam.finding: Rinne positive bilaterally (AC > BC). Weber midline.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms sensorineural loss (not conductive)

physicalExam.finding: Difficulty hearing whispered speech bilaterally, especially at high frequencies.
physicalExam.reasoning: Simple bedside test confirms hearing impairment

physicalExam.finding: CN VIII: decreased hearing bilaterally. Other cranial nerves intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Isolated CN VIII involvement, symmetric

labs.name: Basic metabolic panel
labs.result: Normal. Cr 1.0, glucose 95.
labs.reasoning: Labs generally not helpful for NIHL. Consider if concerned about metabolic causes of hearing loss (diabetes, kidney disease).

labs.name: TSH (if hearing loss unexplained)
labs.result: Normal at 2.1.
labs.reasoning: Hypothyroidism can cause hearing loss. Check if clinical suspicion or hearing loss pattern atypical for NIHL.

imaging.name: MRI brain/IAC with gadolinium
imaging.result: No acoustic neuroma. No cerebellopontine angle mass. No other abnormality.
imaging.reasoning: Consider if asymmetric hearing loss to rule out acoustic neuroma. This case is symmetric, so not strictly required - but often obtained to be thorough.

procedures.name: Pure tone audiometry (DIAGNOSTIC)
procedures.result: Bilateral symmetric sensorineural hearing loss with classic '4 kHz notch': thresholds at 250-2000 Hz: 25-30 dB (mild loss); at 3000-6000 Hz: 55-65 dB (moderately-severe loss); recovery at 8000 Hz: 45 dB. Speech recognition score 76% bilaterally.
procedures.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC test for NIHL. The '4 kHz notch' - maximum loss at 3000-6000 Hz with relative preservation at higher and lower frequencies - is PATHOGNOMONIC for noise-induced hearing loss.

procedures.name: Tympanometry
procedures.result: Type A tympanograms bilaterally - normal middle ear function and compliance.
procedures.reasoning: Confirms normal middle ear function. Rules out conductive component (which would require different workup).

procedures.name: Otoacoustic emissions (OAE)
procedures.result: Absent OAEs at 3000-6000 Hz bilaterally, consistent with cochlear damage.
procedures.reasoning: OAEs assess cochlear hair cell function. Absent at frequencies corresponding to audiometric loss confirms cochlear (sensory) damage.

procedures.name: Auditory brainstem response (ABR) - if asymmetric
procedures.result: Not indicated - hearing loss is symmetric.
procedures.reasoning: ABR useful to rule out retrocochlear pathology (acoustic neuroma) if hearing loss is asymmetric. Not needed for symmetric NIHL.

treatments.name: Hearing aids (TREATMENT)
treatments.result: Bilateral behind-the-ear hearing aids recommended. Significantly improve speech understanding and quality of life.
treatments.reasoning: Hearing aids are the primary treatment for NIHL. Modern digital aids are effective and discrete. Insurance/workers' comp may cover cost.

treatments.name: Hearing protection (ESSENTIAL for prevention)
treatments.result: Fitted for custom-molded ear plugs (25 dB attenuation). Also provided foam plugs and over-ear muffs. Counseled to use EVERY time exposed to loud noise.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL: Must protect remaining hearing. Further exposure will cause additional irreversible damage. Custom plugs more comfortable = better compliance.

treatments.name: Tinnitus management
treatments.result: Tinnitus masking features in hearing aids. White noise machine for sleep. Counseling that tinnitus is not dangerous.
treatments.reasoning: Tinnitus is common with NIHL and can be distressing. Masking, cognitive strategies, and reassurance are helpful. Severe cases may benefit from tinnitus retraining therapy.

treatments.name: Communication strategies counseling
treatments.result: Counseled on: facing speakers, reducing background noise, using closed captions, speech-to-text apps.
treatments.reasoning: Communication strategies improve daily functioning even with hearing aids.

consults.name: Audiology
consults.result: Confirmed NIHL pattern. Fitted for hearing aids. Provided custom ear protection. Scheduled annual audiometric monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Audiology is essential for hearing aid fitting, hearing protection, and monitoring. Annual audiograms detect progression.

consults.name: Occupational Medicine
consults.result: Documents occupational causation. Initiates workers' compensation claim for hearing aids and lost hearing. Recommends workplace hearing conservation program review.
consults.reasoning: NIHL is occupational - patient likely qualifies for workers' compensation. Employer's hearing conservation program should be evaluated.

consults.name: ENT/Otolaryngology
consults.result: Confirmed sensorineural hearing loss. No surgical treatment indicated. Agrees with hearing aids.
consults.reasoning: ENT evaluation confirms no treatable cause (otosclerosis, cholesteatoma, etc.). No surgical options for NIHL - management is hearing aids.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Outpatient management. Hearing aid fitting in 2 weeks. Annual audiometric monitoring.
disposition.reasoning: NIHL is managed entirely as outpatient. Hearing aids, protection, and monitoring.

disposition.name: Workers' compensation claim
disposition.result: Workers' comp claim initiated for occupational hearing loss. May cover hearing aids, medical care, and permanent partial disability.
disposition.reasoning: Occupational disease - patient entitled to workers' compensation benefits for hearing aids and any permanent impairment.

disposition.name: Workplace evaluation
disposition.result: OSHA workplace assessment recommended. Employer must have hearing conservation program if noise >85 dBA.
disposition.reasoning: Employer may not be compliant with OSHA hearing conservation requirements. Coworkers may also be affected and need screening.

disposition.name: Coworker screening recommended
disposition.result: Recommend employer provide baseline and annual audiometry for all noise-exposed workers per OSHA requirements.
disposition.reasoning: If one worker has NIHL, others with similar exposure are at risk. Employer required to provide audiometric testing.

disposition.name: Prevention counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: damage is irreversible, but further damage is preventable. Wear hearing protection at work AND during recreational noise (shooting, concerts, power tools). Every exposure without protection causes additional permanent damage.
disposition.reasoning: Must emphasize prevention - patient still has residual hearing to protect. Both occupational and recreational noise require protection.

disposition.name: Prognosis counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: hearing loss is permanent but will not worsen IF further noise exposure is prevented with protection. Hearing aids significantly improve function. Tinnitus may persist but is manageable.
disposition.reasoning: Set realistic expectations. Damage is permanent but progression is preventable.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Annual audiometry to monitor for progression. Hearing aid adjustment as needed. Earlier follow-up if symptoms change.
disposition.reasoning: Annual monitoring essential to detect any progression and adjust management.

actualDiagnosis.name: Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL)
keyFindings: High-frequency loss pattern (trouble hearing high-pitched voices)
teachingPoints: NIHL is the most common occupational disease - affects millions of workers
The '4 kHz notch' on audiogram is classic: maximum loss at 3-6 kHz with recovery at 8 kHz
Damage is CUMULATIVE and IRREVERSIBLE - prevention is key
OSHA limits: 90 dB for 8 hours, but damage can occur at 85 dB
Every 5 dB increase halves the safe exposure time
Tinnitus often accompanies NIHL and can be disabling
Hearing protection must be worn consistently - even occasional exposure without protection causes damage
Hearing aids can significantly improve quality of life

closureScript.diagnosis: You have noise-induced hearing loss from your years of construction work. All that loud noise over 25 years has damaged the delicate hair cells in your inner ear - especially the ones that detect high-pitched sounds, which is why you have trouble hearing women's and children's voices.
closureScript.plan: The damage that's already done can't be reversed, but we can help you hear better with hearing aids. Modern aids are small and effective. Just as important - you need to start wearing hearing protection consistently to prevent more damage. I'm also referring you to occupational medicine because this may qualify for workers' compensation.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: If you don't start protecting your hearing now, you'll continue to lose more. At your current trajectory, you could end up with severe hearing loss. The ringing is likely permanent, but protecting your hearing will prevent it from getting worse.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know hearing protection is uncomfortable and inconvenient, but the alternative is ending up deaf. There are many different types - foam plugs, custom-molded plugs, over-ear muffs. Find what works for you and wear it every time. The shooting range too.

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.
